question.text: The intrinsic value of an option can:

answer.text: Never be negative (minimum value is zero)
Be negative for put options only
Be negative when volatility is low

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Intrinsic value is max(S-X, 0) for calls and max(X-S, 0) for puts. It can never be negative because the option holder would simply not exercise if doing so would result in a loss. **The Trap:** A negative intrinsic value would mean paying to exercise — no rational buyer would do this. **Cross-Exam Context:** Intrinsic value concept is tested in Series I and VIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Intrinsic value = what you'd get if you exercised right now. Time value = what you pay extra for the possibility of future favorable movement.
